mitey v0.3.0 ▶




A lightweight R package with methods to estimate serial intervals and time-varying reproduction numbers from infectious disease outbreak data.


GitHub CRAN Publication







vacamole ▶




An R package that features a deterministic age-structured compartmental susceptible-exposed-infectious-recovered (SEIR) model and supporting functions.


GitHub Publication







morevac ▶




An R package that features a multi-annual, individual-based, stochastic, force of infection model that accounts for individual exposure histories and disease/vaccine dynamics influencing susceptibility.


GitHub Publication







pika ▶




A lightweight R package for estimating the optimal lag and rolling correlation by grouping variable between two time series.


GitHub Publication







serosolver ▶




An open source R package for inferring epidemiological and immunological dynamics from serological data using a Bayesian framework to reconstruct individual-level infection histories and estimate antibody kinetics.

#' Calculate Required Coffee for Debugging Session
#'
#' @param error_messages Integer. Number of different error messages
#' @param browser_tabs Integer. Troubleshooting browser tabs currently open
#' @param hours_stuck Numeric. Hours spent on current bug
#'
#' @return Integer. Recommended cups of coffee (max 3 for your health)
#'
#' @examples
#' coffee_for_debugging(1, 2, 0.5)
#'
#' @author Caffeinated Coder
#' 
coffee_for_debugging <- function(error_messages,browser_tabs, hours_stuck){
  
  coffee_needed <- error_messages + (browser_tabs * 0.2) + hours_stuck
  result <- min(ceiling(coffee_needed), 3)  # Cap at 3 cups
  
  if (result >= 3) {
    message("Maybe try rubber duck debugging instead?")
  }
  return(result)
}

Writing the help files as you write the code, so they can be documented in real time Future you will thank you!

title: About
text:
I’m Kylie, an Atlanta-native (technically Decatur), crazy dog lady, runner, and science nerd. I work as an infectious disease modeller, meaning that I use math, stats, and R to study the spread of diseases and how interventions, like vaccines, can impact that spread. My career path has taken me from Atlanta to London, Amsterdam, Hong Kong, and soon to Melbourne. I love to explore both near and far, whether that’s through research collaborations, weekend hikes, discovering local coffee shops, or finding the best running trails in each new city.
Scroll through my journey below and watch it unfold on the map.

title: Kylie Ainslie, PhD
text:
Hello!
I’m Kylie Ainslie, and I am the Modelling For Policy Lead at The Peter Doherty Institute for Infection and Immunity at the University of Melbourne (UoM, 2025-Present) and an Honorary Assistant Professor at the University of Hong Kong School of Public Health (2022-Present). At UoM, I lead a large multidisciplinary team that directly informs Australia’s National Immunisation Program. My broader research interests are the development of mathematical and statistical methods to determine how vaccine-induced protection wanes over time and the development of open-source software. At the University of Hong Kong my work focuses on determining the real world protection provided by vaccines against respiratory diseases, such as COVID-19 and influenza.
Previously, I was an Senior Researcher in infectious disease modelling at the Dutch National Institute of Public Health and the Environment (RIVM) (2020-2025), where my work focused on using mathematical models of infectious disease transmission to determine the impact of vaccination strategies on disease spread.
Prior to joining RIVM, I was a postdoctoral researcher at the MRC Centre for Global Infectious Disease Analysis at Imperial College London (2018-2020), where I was involved in modelling infectious disease dynamics, specifically developing individual-based mathematical models of susceptibility to determine how repeated exposures to pathogens (e.g., influenza) and interventions (e.g., vaccines) influence an individual’s susceptibility to subsequent infections. I was also a member of the Imperial COVID-19 Response Team and actively involved in the modelling and characterisation of the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic, including as part of the Real-time Assessment of Community Transmission (REACT) study analytical team.
I obtained a PhD in Biostatistics from Emory University. My doctoral research focused on developing stochastic, agent-based models of infectious diseases and statistical methods to evaluate vaccine effectiveness from observational studies.

title: Pandemic Preparedness & Response
text: Focus: Developing and applying real-time analytical frameworks for outbreak response, from community surveillance to intervention evaluation.

title: Pandemic Preparedness & Response
text: Pandemic preparedness requires robust analytical frameworks that can be rapidly deployed during emerging outbreaks. My work focuses on developing and applying real-time modelling approaches to support public health decision-making during epidemics and pandemics.

title: Pandemic Preparedness & Response
text:
COVID-19 Pandemic Response
During the COVID-19 pandemic, I contributed to several major initiatives providing real-time epidemiological intelligence:

REACT Study (England)
The REal-time Assessment of Community Transmission (REACT) study was one of the largest community surveillance programmes for SARS-CoV-2. Using repeated cross-sectional surveys of random samples of the population in England, we tracked infection prevalence and detected resurgences before they appeared in routine surveillance data.


European Interventions Analysis
As part of the Imperial College COVID-19 Response Team, we estimated the effects of non-pharmaceutical interventions on COVID-19 transmission across 11 European countries. This work, published in Nature, demonstrated that lockdowns substantially reduced transmission and prevented millions of deaths.


China’s Exit from Lockdown
We developed methods to track the success of China’s exit from strict social distancing measures by analyzing the relationship between mobility data and transmission dynamics.

title: Pandemic Preparedness & Response
text:
ESCAPE Project

The ESCAPE (European Surveillance and Control of Antimicrobial Resistant, Pandemic, and Epidemic Diseases) project aims to build European capacity for rapid response to future pandemic threats.
Key objectives:

Developing standardised surveillance protocols
Building modelling capacity across European institutions
Creating frameworks for rapid data sharing during outbreaks
Training the next generation of outbreak responders

title: Transmission Dynamics & Tools
text: Focus: Understanding fundamental characteristics of infectious disease transmission and developing accessible software tools for outbreak analysis.

title: Transmission Dynamics & Tools
text: Understanding the fundamental characteristics of infectious disease transmission is essential for outbreak response and public health planning. My research focuses on estimating key epidemiological parameters and developing accessible software tools that make these methods widely available.

title: Transmission Dynamics & Tools
text:
Serial Interval & Reproduction Number Estimation
The serial interval (time between symptom onset in successive cases) and reproduction number (average number of secondary infections) are fundamental parameters for understanding disease spread. I have developed methods and software for estimating these parameters from outbreak data.

The mitey Package
mitey is a lightweight R package that implements:

The Vink method for serial interval estimation from outbreak data
Methods for estimating time-varying reproduction numbers
Convergence diagnostics and multiple restart optimization for robust estimation

The package is available on CRAN and includes detailed vignettes demonstrating applications to real outbreak data.

title: Transmission Dynamics & Tools
text:
Disease Investigations

Epidemiology of Scabies
Scabies is a contagious skin disease caused by the Sarcoptes scabiei mite, affecting over 400 million people annually worldwide. Despite its prevalence, fundamental epidemiological characteristics have remained poorly described.
Using data from Dutch sentinel surveillance (2011-2023), we provided:


First published estimates of the mean serial interval of scabies infection


Time-varying reproduction numbers showing increasing transmission in recent years


Methods applicable to other neglected tropical diseases




Mpox Transmission
Following the 2022 mpox outbreak in non-endemic regions, we investigated transmission characteristics using detailed exposure histories from cases in the Netherlands.


Serial interval estimates from identified transmission pairs


Evidence of presymptomatic transmission informing isolation guidance


Public health recommendations for contact tracing protocols

title: Modelling for Public Health Policy
text: Focus: Developing and applying mathematical models to evaluate intervention strategies and support evidence-based public health decision-making.

title: Modelling for Public Health Policy
text: Mathematical models of infectious disease transmission are valuable tools for informing public health policy. My work focuses on developing and applying models that can evaluate different intervention scenarios and support evidence-based decision-making.

title: Modelling for Public Health Policy
text:
COVID-19 Vaccination Strategies

Scenario Modelling in the Netherlands
During the COVID-19 pandemic, I led scenario modelling efforts at RIVM (the Dutch National Institute for Public Health and the Environment) to anticipate the impact of vaccination strategies. We developed a deterministic age-structured compartmental model (vacamole) to compare different vaccination scenarios with respect to disease outcomes.


Infections


Hospitalisations


ICU Admissions


Deaths


Key analyses included evaluating the impact of extending vaccination to adolescents and children, comparing different vaccine rollout strategies, and assessing the timing and targeting of booster campaigns.


European Scenario Modelling Hub
I contributed to the European COVID-19 Scenario Modelling Hub, a collaborative initiative that brought together modelling teams across Europe to provide ensemble projections for policy planning.
This work demonstrated the value of multi-model approaches for capturing uncertainty in epidemic projections and informed public health planning across multiple countries.

title: Modelling for Public Health Policy
text:
Intervention Analysis
Beyond vaccination, my policy modelling work extends to evaluating non-pharmaceutical interventions and their timing. This includes:

Optimal timing of intervention implementation
Trade-offs between intervention stringency and duration
Regional coordination of public health measures

title: Vaccine Effectiveness Methods
text: Focus: Developing and evaluating statistical methods that provide reliable vaccine effectiveness estimates while accounting for biases in observational study designs.

title: Vaccine Effectiveness Methods
text: Estimating vaccine effectiveness (VE) from observational studies presents unique methodological challenges. My research focuses on developing and evaluating statistical methods that can provide reliable VE estimates while accounting for the biases inherent in different study designs.

title: Vaccine Effectiveness Methods
text:
Evaluation of Waning Vaccine Effectiveness (WAVE)
The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the critical need for robust methods to assess how vaccine protection changes over time. The WAVE project addresses this gap by:

Developing methods to rigorously compare approaches for assessing waning vaccine-induced protection
Disentangling true from perceived waning - distinguishing actual decline in immunity from changes in the circulating pathogen or population
Identifying determinants of waning protection, including how individual characteristics influence the rate of decline
Applications across multiple vaccines including pertussis, influenza, and COVID-19

title: Vaccine Effectiveness Methods
text:
Influenza Vaccine Effectiveness

Bias in Observational Studies
I developed dynamic transmission models to evaluate sources of bias in observational VE studies, particularly the test-negative design. This work identified conditions under which test-negative studies may over- or underestimate true VE, providing guidance for study design and interpretation.


Transmission Blocking
Using maximum likelihood estimation, I developed methods to estimate VE against both:

Susceptibility to infection
Transmission to others

These methods use household surveillance data to separately estimate these two components of vaccine protection.


Repeat Vaccination Effects
Annual influenza vaccination raises questions about how prior vaccination affects current season protection. Using individual-based stochastic models (morevac), I investigated optimal vaccination strategies in children, accounting for complex exposure histories and antigenic dynamics.

title: Scabies, who cares?
text:
Sarcoptes scabiei




Scabies is a disease caused by infestation of the skin with a microscopic mite.


a.k.a. the Itch or seven-year itch.

Symptoms are characterized by itchiness and rash at the site of infestation.

title: Automating Data Pipelines with Maeestro
text:
Having just successfully scheduled my first data pipeline to run automatically with maestro and cron made me realise that I hadn’t found a good tutorial for the whole process. It was a bit of a learning curve, but once I got everything set up in the correct way, it actually seemed pretty straightforward (always the case in hindsight, right?).
Where I struggled on previous attempts at automation was that I misunderstood how the automation occurrs. It wasn’t until I read the last sentence of the Maestro Quick Start Guide that I understood where I had gone wrong:

“Importantly, it isn’t maestro’s job to actually run [the pipeline] this often - it’s your job to make sure [the pipeline] runs at that frequency (e.g., deploying it via cron or some cloud environment where code can be scheduled).”

I originally thought that maestro not only orchestrated the pipelines, but also ran them at the specified schedule. This is not the case. So, for all those who are also struggling, here is the process I used to automate my pipeline. I’m sure a better tutorial exists, but because I couldn’t find it in a 30 second google, I decided to write one.


Maestro is an R package designed to organize and orchestrate complex pipelines. It handles tasks like dependencies, scheduling logic, and defining pipeline steps, but maestro does not manage when the pipeline executes. This is where cron (or other schedulers) comes into play.
cron is a Unix-based utility for task scheduling. It allows you to specify when a task (like running a maestro pipeline) should execute. cron periodically triggers the execution of a script, ensuring the pipeline runs at the desired frequency. cronis not the only task scheduler, there are a lot. While doing a quick search to check for other task schedulers, I just discovered there are two R packages cronR (for Linux/macOS) and taskscheduleR (for Windows) that are designed to make this process easier. Wish I’d known about them a few hours ago. Oh well!
So essentially, maestro using a YAML file ensures tasks within the pipeline are executed in the correct order and retries failed tasks as needed. Whereas, cron periodically runs a shell script that triggers the maestro pipeline. More on shell scripts in a bit!



I have my data pipeline and orchestration files inside an R package that I created, so the set-up might be slightly different that stand alone scripts in a directory. Normally, I would link to the git repo, so that anyone looking at this can see all my code and my set-up, but in this case, it’s part of some consulting work that is private.

title: Automating Data Pipelines with Maeestro
text:
Steps for Automating the Pipeline

1. Create a YAML file
The first step is to create a YAML file that defines the schedule, tasks, and notifications for the pipeline. Here’s an example:
name: my_data_pipeline
schedule:
  type: cron
  cron: "0 0 1,15 * *"  # Runs on the 1st and 15th of every month at midnight
start_time: "2024-12-01 00:00:00"
task:
  script: Rscript -e 'my_package::my_data_pipeline()'
  working_dir: ~/Documents/my_package
  retry_policy:
    retries: 3
    delay: 5m
notifications:
  on_success:
    - type: email
      to: my.email@gmail.com
  on_failure:
    - type: email
      to: my.email@gmail.com
The name field should be a descriptive name for your pipeline. The schedule field specifies when and how often the task should run, and using what scheduling mechanism (here, type: cron). start_time specifies the first time the pipeline is allowed to run, (here, December 1, 2024 at 12:00 AM). The task field defines the details of the task to execute. Specifically, script specifies the command to run the pipeline, working_dir specifies the directory to execute the script from, retry_policy specifies how retries are handled if the task fails, retries denotes the maximum number of retries (here, 3), and delay specifies the time delay between retries (here, 5 minutes). The notifications field configures notifications for task outcomes. Here, we configure maestro to send an email when the task succeeds (on_success) and when the task fails (on_failure).


2. Wrap your pipeline into a function
Ensure that your pipeline is encapsulated within a function, such as my_package::my_data_pipeline(), so that it can be executed with the appropriate arguments. There’s a good explanation of how to do this in the maestro README. Below is an example I took from the above README.

#' Example ETL pipeline
#' @maestroFrequency 1 day
#' @maestroStartTime 2024-03-25 12:30:00
my_etl <- function() {
  
  # Pretend we're getting data from a source
  message("Get data")
  extracted <- mtcars
  
  # Transform
  message("Transforming")
  transformed <- extracted |> 
    dplyr::mutate(hp_deviation = hp - mean(hp))
  
  # Load - write to a location
  message("Writing")
  write.csv(transformed, file = paste0("transformed_mtcars_", Sys.Date(), ".csv"))
}

All of the pipeline tasks are wrapped in a function called my_etl(), which has some maestro-specific roxygen-style comments, namely @maestroFrequency and @maestroStartTimewhich indicate how frequently the pipeline should run and when it should strat, respectively. For more details, see here.


3. Automate the pipeline using cron
Next, you’ll need to create a Shell script and set up cron to run your pipeline at the specified frequencies.

Step 3.1: Create a .sh file
Create a .sh file by first opening a text editor using the Mac terminal. I used the nano editor.

nano my_script.sh

Add the text to the .sh file to execute the pipeline using Rscript:

#!/bin/bash
Rscript -e "system('maestro run /path/to/maestro.yaml')"

If editing the text to the .sh file directly in the terminal with nano:

Press CTRL + O to save. (the letter “O”, not zero)
Press Enter to confirm the file name.
Press CTRL + X to exit.



Step 3.2: Make the .sh file executable
Run the following command to make the script executable:

chmod +x /path/to/run_pipeline.sh



Step 3.3: Schedule the pipeline with cron
Open your crontab configuration using crontab -e in the terminal and adding a line to schedule the pipeline to run at the desired frequency. For example, to run it daily at midnight:

0 0 * * * /path/to/run_pipeline.sh

The cron schedule is defined by five fields followed by the command to execute:

* * * * * command_to_run
- - - - -
| | | | |
| | | | +--- Day of the week (0 - 7, where both 0 and 7 represent Sunday)
| | | +----- Month (1 - 12)
| | +------- Day of the month (1 - 31)
| +--------- Hour (0 - 23)
+----------- Minute (0 - 59)

The different fields are defined as: 1. Minute: Specifies the minute of the hour (0-59). 2. Hour: Specifies the hour of the day (0-23, 24-hour clock). 3. Day of the Month: Specifies the day of the month (1-31). 4. Month: Specifies the month (1-12, or names like jan, feb). 5. Day of the Week: Specifies the day of the week (0-7, where both 0 and 7 represent Sunday).
Special characters can be used to indicated: - *: Wildcard that means “every” (e.g., every minute, every hour). - ,: List separator (e.g., 1,15 means the 1st and 15th). - -: Range (e.g., 1-5 means days 1 through 5). - /: Step values (e.g., */5 means every 5 units, like every 5 minutes).
Here are some examples:

30 14 * * 1-5 /path/to/script.sh: Runs at 2:30 PM, Monday through Friday.
0 0 1,15 * * /path/to/script.sh: Runs at midnight on the 1st and 15th of the month.
*/10 * * * * /path/to/script.sh: Runs every 10 minutes.
0 8-18/2 * * 1-5 /path/to/script.sh: Runs every 2 hours between 8 AM and 6 PM, Monday to Friday.



Step 3.4: Verify the cron job
Once you’ve configured your chrontab, you can check that the cron job has been scheduled successfully by running:

crontab -l




4. Run the pipeline manually
Before waiting for the first scheduled runtime, test that everything is set up correctly by manually running the .sh script in the terminal:

/path/to/run_pipeline.sh 



5. Wait for the scheduled runtime
Once everything is working and verified, you can sit back and wait for the first official scheduled runtime. The pipeline will run automatically based on the cron schedule you’ve defined!

title: Automating Data Pipelines with Maeestro
text:
Conclusion
That’s it! After setting up the YAML file, wrapping the pipeline in a function, creating the .sh file, and scheduling the job with cron, your pipeline should run automatically at the intervals you’ve defined. Remember, maestro handles the pipeline’s internal orchestration, but cron or another scheduling system is what ensures the pipeline is triggered at the right frequency.

title: Vaccine Effectiveness Methods

WAVE Project

Evaluation of Waning Vaccine Effectiveness
The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the critical need for robust methods to assess how vaccine protection changes over time. The WAVE project addresses this gap through rigorous methodological development.


Method Development
Rigorously compare approaches for assessing waning vaccine-induced protection


True vs Perceived Waning
Distinguish actual immunity decline from changes in pathogen or population


Individual Determinants
Identify how characteristics influence rate of protection decline


Multi-Disease Application
Methods applicable across multiple vaccine types

mitey is a lightweight R package for estimating key epidemiological parameters from outbreak data.


Vink Method Serial interval estimation from outbreak data with mixture models


Time-Varying R Reproduction number estimation tracking transmission over time


Robust Fitting Multiple restarts & convergence diagnostics for reliable estimates


Well Documented Vignettes with real-world examples and validation



Documentation CRAN GitHub

title: Scabies, who cares?
text:
Sarcoptes scabiei




Scabies is a disease caused by infestation of the skin with a microscopic mite.


a.k.a. the Itch or seven-year itch.




From the Latin scabere, meaning ‘to scratch’.






Symptoms are characterized by itchiness and rash at the site of infestation.







Scabies mite. Image Credit: Aliaksei Marozau / Shutterstock.com

The global disease burden of scabies is low compared to other infectious diseases
Disease severity is also low

title: Scabies, who cares?
text:
But…
During periods of high scabies prevalence:

Higher burden due to secondary infections
Increased pressure on healthcare systems
Negative economic impacts
Stigma-related delays in care-seeking

title: Scabies, who cares?
text:
Serial Interval

We calculate the index case-to-case (ICC) interval for each person,

index case: the person with the greatest value for number of days since symptom onset.
“secondary” cases: have an ICC interval calculated as the number of days between their symptom onset day and the index case.

title: Scabies, who cares?
text:
Serial Interval

We use a maximum likelihood framework to determine how likely each ICC interval can be attributed to each transmission route - We then estimate serial interval using ICC intervals consistent with Primary-Secondary transmission

title: Scabies, who cares?
text:
Basic Reproduction Number \(R_0\)

We can relate the growth rate to the basic reproduction number as \[R_0 = \exp(r*T – (1/2) r^2 s^2)\]

\(r\) is the annual growth rate,
\(T\) is the mean generation time (in years), and
\(s^2\) is the variance of the generation time distribution.




Assumption: nearly everyone exposed has not been previously infected.

title: Scabies, who cares?
text:
Basic Reproduction Number \(R_0\)

We can relate the growth rate to the basic reproduction number as \[R_0 = \exp(r*T – (1/2) r^2 s^2)\]

\(r\) is the annual growth rate,
\(T\) is the mean generation time serial interval (in years), and
\(s^2\) is the variance of the generation time serial interval distribution.



This gives us an \(R_0 = 1.09\) \((95\% CI: 1.07, 1.11)\)



Assumption: nearly everyone exposed has not been previously infected.

Policy Implications

Controlling 8% of secondary infections prevents epidemic growth




 Educational campaign targeting high-risk groups




 Enhanced GP training for rapid diagnosis




 Voluntary screening




 Systematic contact tracing




 Targeted (voluntary) mass drug administration campaigns

title: Scabies, who cares?
text:
This is only the beginning

There is still a lot that we don’t know



Future research should prioritize three key areas:





collecting transmission pair data




characterizing the delays between symptom onset and healthcare seeking




reproducing Mellanby’s 1944 incubation period estimates

title: I Didn’t Set Out to Be a Software Developer
section: [VISUAL: Single question on a dark slide, centred, nothing else]
text:
[VISUAL: Single question on a dark slide, centred, nothing else]

How many of you consider yourself a software developer?


Let it sit. Then: “Good. Neither do I.”

title: I Didn’t Set Out to Be a Software Developer
section: [VISUAL: Personal photo — you at work, or in the Netherlands]
text:
[VISUAL: Personal photo — you at work, or in the Netherlands]

“In June 2021, I was sitting in my office in the Netherlands and my boss walked in and asked me a question I was not prepared for.”
Pause.
“He said: should we vaccinate children against COVID-19?”
Pause.
“I had two weeks to answer it.”
Let that land.
“I’m not a software developer. I’m an infectious disease modeler. But the reason I was able to deliver that answer on time — and the reason it survived public scrutiny and became part of European-wide disease modelling infrastructure — had almost nothing to do with my epidemiology.”
Pause.
“It had to do with how I set up my project folder.”
Pause.
“What I got back from that choice was not what I expected.”

Speed


🔄 Models updated rapidly as new data came in
⏰ Analysis delivered on time
🛡️ Survived a public audit
💉 Directly informed national health policy


“I wasn’t trying to be organised for its own sake. I was trying to hit a deadline. The structure is what made that possible.”

“There’s a method for estimating serial intervals — how quickly a disease spreads from person to person. It was published in 2014. Solid method. But using it meant digging through a paper, reimplementing the algorithm yourself, hoping you got it right.”
“Nobody was using it. Not because it wasn’t good. Because it wasn’t accessible.”

title: I Didn’t Set Out to Be a Software Developer
text:
The unexpected benefit

Reach


📄 Method existed only inside a paper
📦 Now accessible to any epidemiologist in the world
🔬 Research became a tool, not just a publication


“I didn’t set out to democratise a method. I was just trying to make my own analysis reproducible. Packaging it was the obvious next step — and it meant the method finally had a life beyond the paper it was published in.”

title: I Didn’t Set Out to Be a Software Developer

Transition: “This is where it gets unexpected.”

title: Elevating your research through software development
text:
[VISUAL: Screenshot of rwarehouse.netlify.app]

“This is where it gets unexpected, even for me.”
“I kept running into the same frustration. I knew R could do what I needed. I just couldn’t find the right package. There are over 23,000 packages on CRAN — how do you find the one you need if you don’t already know it exists?”
“And I realised — this wasn’t just my problem. This was everyone’s problem.”
Pause.
“And I had the confidence to do something about it.”

title: I Didn’t Set Out to Be a Software Developer
section: [VISUAL: Search bar / AI semantic search demo screenshot]
text:
[VISUAL: Search bar / AI semantic search demo screenshot]

“With AI-powered semantic search. You describe what you want to do — in plain language — and it finds the tools.”
“I felt confident enough to build this because I had been gradually improving my software development skills. Each project built on the last.”

title: I Didn’t Set Out to Be a Software Developer
text:
The unexpected benefit

Possibility


💡 Hit a problem
🛠️ Realised I had the skills to solve it
🤖 Built an AI-powered tool for the R community
🧑‍🤝‍🧑 For researchers, by a researcher


“I didn’t set out to build an AI-powered tool. I set out to organise a folder. But somewhere along the way, without really noticing, I became someone who could.”

title: I Didn’t Set Out to Be a Software Developer
section: [VISUAL: Simple three-step arc — organise → share → build]
text:
[VISUAL: Simple three-step arc — organise → share → build]

Organise → your work becomes faster and more robust
Share → your work gets a life beyond the paper
Build → you become someone who can solve problems you didn’t know you could solve


“None of this required becoming a software developer. It required borrowing a few ideas from software developers.”

title: I Didn’t Set Out to Be a Software Developer
section: The unexpected benefit of all of it
text:
The unexpected benefit of all of it

Confidence


“Not confidence in software. Confidence in what you’re capable of. That’s the thing I didn’t see coming.”

A PhD dissertation that was a mess
